cards.back: Templates allow writing functions or classes using placeholder types, so the compiler can create identical versions using different types (like a stencil).

cards.text: All template functions or classes must start with {{c1::template <...>}} containing {{c2::template type parameters or expression parameters}}.

cards.front: Why can't you split template class definitions and implementations into .h and .cpp files?
cards.back: Templates are stencils, not actual code. The compiler needs the full definition to instantiate them. Splitting causes linker errors because the .cpp file doesn't know what types to instantiate.

cards.front: What is the difference between full and partial template specialization?
cards.back:
Full specialization: all template parameters are specialized
Partial specialization: only some parameters are specialized
Note: Functions cannot be partially specialized (classes only).

cards.text: Template type parameters are typically named {{c1::T, T1, T2}} or other {{c2::single letter names}} like S.

cards.front: Name two C++ standard library classes that use templates.
cards.back: std::array and std::vector (also std::string, std::map, std::set, etc.).

cards.concept: Why templates are ideal for container classes
cards.explanation:
Container classes (arrays, vectors, lists, maps) need to work with many different data types. Templates allow:
  • Writing the container logic once
  • Type safety (compile-time checking)
  • No code duplication
  • Zero runtime overhead compared to hand-written versions

cards.front: What types can template expression (non-type) parameters be?
cards.back:
Usually integral types, but also:
  • Enumerations
  • Pointers/references to functions or objects
  • std::nullptr_t
  • Floating point (C++20+)

cards.question: Write a template class Pair with two different template types T and S.
cards.answer:
template <typename T, typename S>
class Pair {
private:
T m_first;
S m_second;
public:
Pair(const T& first, const S& second)
: m_first{first}, m_second{second} {}
T& first() { return m_first; }
S& second() { return m_second; }
};
cards.explanation: Separate the two types by comma in the template parameter declaration. Each member uses its respective type.

cards.text: Template specialization can override {{c1::default behavior}} for {{c2::specific types}}, but prefer {{c3::non-template functions}} when possible for function templates.
Template class code should be kept in {{c1::header files}} with {{c2::implementations underneath the class}}.
Templates are often used for {{c1::container classes}} so a container can be written {{c2::once}} and used with any appropriate type.
